notes: From uk.xtool.com official regional material settings table (mirrors main xtool.com library content for D1 Pro). D1 Pro 20W is generally able to cut 3mm basswood plywood in a single pass; exact figure taken from official settings table.
Vector engrave setting for 3mm basswood plywood, D1 Pro 20W, from official uk.xtool.com material settings table (regional mirror of xTool's official material library).

Derived from official uk.xtool.com D1 Pro table pattern (5W/10W acrylic rows explicitly show 10W needing ~3-4mm/s at 3-4 passes for full-power cuts on similarly-dense material); basswood-specific 10W row not explicitly captured in scraped excerpt, so cross-referenced against the machine's known power scaling from 20W settings. Verify directly against uk.xtool.com/pages/material-settings D1 Pro tab before production use.

Community-tested aggregator (bonnycreations.com) settings page for D1 Pro 20W; page explicitly states values are community-tested, not official xTool figures. Listed among '5mm Plywood' entries. Always test on scrap first per source's own disclaimer.

Community-tested aggregator estimate for 5mm plywood bitmap/raster engrave on D1 Pro 20W; exact numeric row for this specific thickness/operation combination was not directly visible in the scraped excerpt and is extrapolated from adjacent plywood entries on the same page. Treat as a starting point only, not a verified figure.

xTool official support article 1716 confirms Basswood Plywood is compatible with S1 for both engraving and cutting (checkmark compatibility table) but does not itself publish exact power/speed numbers -- article directs users to the xTool Material Settings Library (materials.xtool.com / mt.xtool.com) for exact figures. Numeric values here are reasonable estimates based on comparable D1 Pro 20W cut settings scaled for S1's enclosed design; confirm exact numbers in-app via S1's built-in material library before cutting.

xTool official article 2877 covers S1 flat-surface processing thickness limits and confirms plywood-class materials are supported within the machine's flat-processing thickness range, but does not itemize this exact power/speed/pass combination. Estimate based on 40W being roughly double the 20W module's cutting power at comparable material thickness.

No exact published S1 40W bitmap-engrave figure found for 5mm basswood plywood; estimated from general S1 material compatibility guidance (article 2877) and comparable D1 Pro/P2 engrave ratios. Run a Material Test Array before committing to a project.

xTool official P2 FAQ (article 825) confirms P2's CO2 laser can cut wood up to 18mm and plywood up to 12mm as a general capability statement, but does not give a specific 3mm basswood plywood power/speed/pass row. Numeric values here are estimated from third-party P2 test data (machinesformakers.com, hobbylasercutters.com) for thin basswood-class plywood, scaled to a conservative single-pass cut. Confidence is medium because the machine capability ceiling is officially confirmed even though this exact row is not.

Legacy xTool support article (now decommissioned zendesk mirror, content pointer redirects to support.xtool.com) lists Basswood Plywood 6mm laser-cut parameters in its material parameter table. Zendesk page displays banner stating site is decommissioned and content may not be up to date -- treat as historical reference, cross-check current figures via xTool Material Settings Library before use.

From the same legacy (decommissioned) zendesk material parameter table as the corresponding cut row above; bitmap engrave setting for 6mm basswood plywood on P2. Flagged medium confidence due to the page's decommissioned status.

Pattern derived from the M1 10W Pine Plywood cut row on the same official uk.xtool.com table (100% / 4mm-s-equivalent / 1 pass structure) scaled to D1 Pro 20W's higher power module; direct D1 Pro-specific pine plywood row was not captured verbatim in the excerpt retrieved. Cross-check exact row in-app.

From legacy decommissioned zendesk material parameter table; Cherry Plywood 3mm listed among the wood species covered. Numeric row reconstructed from partial table data -- confirm current figures via xTool Material Settings Library (materials.xtool.com) since this source is explicitly marked outdated.

From legacy decommissioned zendesk material parameter table covering Walnut Plywood at 3mm/6mm/12mm; this row is the 3mm bitmap engrave entry. Page is officially marked decommissioned by xTool with a redirect notice to support.xtool.com -- treat as historical reference only.

Black Acrylic 3mm cut setting for D1 Pro 40W module scaled proportionally down is NOT used here -- this row uses the directly observed D1 Pro table pattern for opaque black/dark acrylic cut settings. Per official support.xtool.com/article/555, cast acrylic is generally better suited to laser ENGRAVING while extruded acrylic is better suited to laser CUTTING -- for cutting tasks, extruded acrylic is the recommended acrylic type; this cast-acrylic cut row is included for completeness but extruded is preferred for cutting per xTool's own guidance.

Extruded acrylic is xTool's officially recommended acrylic type for laser CUTTING per support.xtool.com/article/555 ('Essentials When Processing Acrylic with xTool Laser Machine'): 'Acrylic is normally divided into extruded and cast acrylic. In most cases, cast acrylic is ideal for laser engraving, while extruded acrylic is ideal for laser cutting.' Power/speed/pass values from uk.xtool.com official D1 Pro Black Acrylic table (labelled generically as 'Acrylic', color/opacity type not always distinguished from cast in the source table -- treat exact pass count as approximate).

Cast acrylic is xTool's officially recommended acrylic type for laser ENGRAVING per support.xtool.com/article/555: 'cast acrylic is ideal for laser engraving.' Same source also warns: with the blue diode laser you CANNOT cut or engrave clear/white/blue acrylic because it is transparent to the 450nm wavelength -- engrave dark-colored, non-transparent acrylic only. Numeric power/speed cross-referenced against uk.xtool.com D1 Pro Black Acrylic (Opaque, Glossy) raster engrave row.

S1 uses a blue diode laser like D1 Pro; per official article 555, clear/transparent acrylic CANNOT be reliably cut with a blue diode laser because the beam is not absorbed and stays unfocused as it passes through transparent material. This row is included only to explicitly document that limitation -- xTool's own guidance is to avoid this combination entirely and use dark, non-transparent acrylic instead, or use a CO2/fiber machine (e.g., P2) for transparent acrylic cutting. Do not treat these numbers as usable; they are a placeholder illustrating the failure case per official guidance.

P2's CO2 laser (10600nm wavelength) is absorbed by transparent acrylic (unlike blue diode lasers), making it the officially recommended machine type for cutting clear acrylic. Official P2 FAQ (article 825) confirms max transparent acrylic cut thickness of 20mm as a general capability figure but does not publish this specific 3mm row; numeric values estimated from third-party P2 test data (machinesformakers.com Transparent Acrylic settings table) scaled to a thin 3mm sheet.

Third-party reviewer (machinesformakers.com) published a Transparent Acrylic settings table for P2 based on their own hands-on testing, not an official xTool source. No official support.xtool.com page was found with this exact engrave row. Treat as a starting point only.

Vector engrave/score-style setting for Black Acrylic 3mm on D1 Pro 40W from official uk.xtool.com material settings table ('Vector engrave | D1 Pro 40W | 25 / 50'). Table did not explicitly separate a distinct 'score' pass count in the source row -- pass assumed to be 1 per typical vector/score convention.

Derived from official uk.xtool.com '3.5mm Corrugated Paper' cut row ('Cut | 100 / 25 / 1' in Power% / Speed(mm/s) / Pass format) applied to standard ~4mm corrugated cardboard. Thickness in this row is an approximation since the source specifically lists 3.5mm.
Raster/bitmap engrave setting for corrugated cardboard from official uk.xtool.com table ('3.5mm Corrugated Paper | Raster engrave | 22 / 260 / 1'). Thickness rounded from 3.5mm source value.

support.xtool.com article 1716 confirms Cardboard is on the S1 material compatibility checklist but does not publish exact numbers; this row is estimated by proportionally scaling the D1 Pro 20W corrugated cardboard cut settings to S1's enclosed 20W module (same power class, similar diode laser type). Confirm via in-app S1 material library before use.

Official xTool Leather Engraving Operation Guide (article 2455) names D1 Pro as a recommended machine for leather work and describes the workflow (focus, trial run on scrap leather, secure with riser base + honeycomb panel + magnets) but explicitly does not publish exact power/speed numbers in the article body -- it recommends running your own trial/test cut on scrap leather first. This row's numeric values are estimated from comparable third-party D1 Pro leather-cutting data (bonnycreations.com) and should be verified with a scrap test per xTool's own explicit guidance.

No exact published D1 Pro leather bitmap-engrave figures found in official sources; official guide (article 2455) instructs users to run their own scrap trial to dial in settings rather than publishing a table. Estimated starting point only -- see xTool's official leather engraving guide for the recommended empirical testing workflow before committing to a project.

S1 material compatibility table (article 1716) confirms Leather is supported for engraving on S1, citing xTool's own caution to consult the Material Settings Library for exact current parameters rather than relying on any static table. Numeric values are estimated from bonnycreations.com S1 40W leather engrave data, which the aggregator itself sources partly from brildor.com and xtool.com/blogs/xtool-academy leather engraving guidance.

Same S1 compatibility source as above (article 1716, confirms Leather cutting compatibility) but no exact figures published officially. Numeric values estimated from bonnycreations.com S1 20W settings page. Always test on scrap leather first.

Official Leather Engraving Operation Guide (article 2455) names P2's CO2 laser as 'a perfect choice' for leather work alongside D1 Pro/M1, but again does not publish a specific numeric table -- recommends scrap trial runs. Estimated from third-party P2 leather test data; verify with your own material test array.

Official uk.xtool.com material settings table, '70g Kraft paper | Cut | 12 / 3 / 1' (Power% / Speed-mm/s / Pass format). Note this particular row appears in a machine-generic subsection of the table (not explicitly re-labeled per D1 Pro/S1/etc for every weight); cross-check the D1 Pro-specific tab if a different figure is needed for your exact machine.
Official uk.xtool.com table, '70g Kraft paper | Raster engrave | 1 / 330 / 1'. Very low power is expected/correct for thin kraft paper to avoid burn-through during engrave.
Official uk.xtool.com D1 Pro 40W table row: 'Kraft Paper | 0.23mm | Cut | D1 Pro 40W | 60 / 60 / 1'.

Official uk.xtool.com D1 Pro 40W table row: 'Kraft Paper | 0.23mm | Raster engrave | D1 Pro 40W | 8 / 80 (100 Lines per cm)'. 100 lines/cm converted to DPI: 100 lines/cm = 254 DPI per official xTool conversion documented in support.xtool.com/article/789.

S1 compatibility table (article 1716) confirms Kraft Paper / paper materials are supported for both engraving and cutting, but exact numbers are not published in that article (it points to the Material Settings Library instead). Estimated from machinesformakers.com S1 Kraft Paper settings page, a third-party test source.

From legacy decommissioned zendesk material parameter table's Corrugated Paper/Kraft Paper section; the CO2 laser on P2 requires much lower power than diode machines for thin paper due to different absorption characteristics. Page explicitly marked decommissioned by xTool -- treat as historical reference.

Community-tested aggregator (bonnycreations.com) lists 'Slate (coaster) engrave: Speed 2200 mm/min, Power 90-90%, Passes 1, DPI 400' -- converted here from mm/min to mm/s (2200/60 = 36.67, rounded to 36). Page notes 'Higher DPI for fine detail and photo work on slate coasters.' Not an official xTool figure; slate is noted elsewhere (support.xtool.com/article/3235, a video tutorial) as requiring careful power control since 'higher power will crack the fine details' per a related xTool community project guide.

Official uk.xtool.com D1 Pro 40W table: 'Rock Coaster | 7mm | Vector engrave | D1 Pro 40W | 10 / 25'. Listed as 'Vector engrave' in the source but functionally equivalent to a score/outline pass at this low power; included as score per this dataset's operation taxonomy.
Official uk.xtool.com D1 Pro 40W table: 'Rock Coaster | 7mm | Raster engrave | D1 Pro 40W | 15 / 120 (100 Lines per cm)'.

From an official xTool community project page (projects.xtool.com, hosted on xTool's own domain) titled 'From Bean to Earth,' using M1 Ultra to engrave natural slate coasters. Settings given as 'POWER-35%, SPEED-130, MODE-GREYSCALE, LINES/CM-300' (alternate option given: 'POWER-30%, SPEED-150'). Speed units not explicitly labeled mm/s in source but consistent with xTool's typical mm/s convention; project explicitly warns 'higher power will crack the fine details' on slate, and recommends low power + high speed over multiple passes if extra depth is needed. Lines/cm of 300 converts to approximately 762 DPI (300 x 2.54).

F1's official IR laser is 2W (support.xtool.com/article/789, article/1947 both confirm 'Blue light (10W) and IR (2W)' for base F1 -- NOT 20W as sometimes seen on F1 Ultra pages). No official support.xtool.com page publishes exact power/speed numbers for metal marking; official guidance (article 1947) explicitly directs users to run their own Material Test Array. This row's power/speed values are from an UNOFFICIAL third-party fan site (xtoolf1.com, not xtool.com or support.xtool.com) and should be treated as an unverified community starting point only. IMPORTANT: official article 1716 also warns that highly reflective materials like stainless steel risk damaging the diode laser module via reflection -- exercise caution and ensure proper eye/equipment protection.

Uses the F1's 2W fiber/IR laser module (per official support.xtool.com/article/789: 'F1 supports two types of laser light sources... Blue light (10W) and IR (2W)'). Numeric power/speed values sourced from an unofficial fan site (xtoolf1.com); a second unofficial fan site (xtoolf1ultra.com, for the F1 Ultra's 20W fiber module) gives a different range (40-60% power, 1000-2000mm/s) for the SAME anodized-aluminum-anodize-removal effect -- the two unofficial sources roughly agree on speed order-of-magnitude but not exactly, and neither is an xTool-official published number. No official support.xtool.com numeric table was found for this operation on either F1 or F1 Ultra; official guidance is to use the in-software Material Test Array.

Official support.xtool.com/article/1326 confirms F1 Ultra's fiber infrared laser is 20W ('blue-light laser (20 W) and fiber infrared laser (20 W)') and that fiber IR 'is ideal for engraving metals such as stainless steel, gold, silver, copper, aluminum' -- but the article gives only process parameters (dot duration 100-500us, Jarvis bitmap mode recommended for IR) and explicitly does NOT publish a power%/speed/pass numeric table for this or any specific metal. The numeric power/speed values here are averaged from two unofficial fan sites (xtoolf1ultra.com: 60-80% power/800-1200mm/s for dark mark; xtoolf1.com: 80-100%/800-1200mm/s) which broadly agree on the speed range but differ somewhat on power. Treat as an unverified starting point only -- always run the official Material Test Array first, and note article 1716's official caution that reflective metals risk damaging a diode laser module via reflection (this specific caution is stated for the diode module, not the fiber module, but general reflection caution applies).

Same official source as above (article 1326) confirms F1 Ultra fiber laser (20W) is suited to aluminum engraving in general terms only. Numeric power/speed values are an average of two unofficial fan-site tables (xtoolf1ultra.com: 40-60%/1000-2000mm/s; xtoolf1.com: 40-60%/1500-2000mm/s) -- these two sources roughly agree with each other, giving somewhat more confidence than the stainless steel row, but neither is an official xTool number. Effect described as removing the anodized layer to reveal the bare aluminum base beneath, producing light-on-dark contrast.

Official uk.xtool.com F1 table row for Black acrylic-adjacent bitmap engrave settings structure ('Black acrylic | 3.0 | Bitmap engrave | 2W infrared laser | 85 / - / 1 | 550 [DPI] | / | 200 [dot duration us] | Jarvis [bitmap mode] | Invert [edit image]'); speed intentionally null here because bitmap engrave on F1's galvo system is governed by Dot Duration (200us) rather than a simple traversal speed, per official support.xtool.com/article/789 parameter logic. This row documents the IR/fiber module parameter structure; treat power/DPI/dot-duration as the primary tunable parameters for F1 bitmap engraves rather than 'speed'.

Official uk.xtool.com F1 table row: 'Black acrylic | 3.0 | Vector cut | 10W blue-light laser | 100 / 2 / 1'. Uses F1's blue diode module (10W), not the fiber/IR module -- consistent with official guidance that cutting is a diode-laser function while metal marking is a fiber-laser function on this dual-laser machine.

Official uk.xtool.com F1 table row: 'Black acrylic | 3.0 | Vector score | 2W infrared laser | 80 / 60 / 1'. Notably uses the 2W IR/fiber module for scoring rather than the blue diode module used for cutting the same material (see companion cut row above) -- an interesting official divergence worth flagging for users.
Official uk.xtool.com F1 table row: 'Black acrylic | 3.0 | Vector engrave | 2W infrared laser | 90 / 200 / 1' with 'Lines per cm: 200' noted in the same row (equivalent to roughly 508 DPI).
Official uk.xtool.com M1 table row: 'Basswood plywood | 3.0 | Laser cutting | M1 5W | 100 / 3 / 1'.

Official uk.xtool.com M1 table row: 'Basswood plywood | 3.0 | Tool cutting | M1 10W | 100 / 4 / 1' (source table labels this operation 'Tool cutting,' interpreted here as equivalent to laser cutting for the 10W module based on table structure/context).
Official uk.xtool.com M1 table row: 'Basswood plywood | 3.0 | Laser bitmap engraving | M1 10W | 70 / 175 / 1'.

CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE: this is a BLADE cut, not a laser cut -- power_pct of 0 correctly reflects that the laser is OFF for this operation. Official uk.xtool.com table: 'PU Heat Transfer Vinyl | 0.2 | Blade cut | M1 10W | 0 / 80 / 1'. M1 and M1 Ultra are hybrid machines with a physical cutting blade specifically so that PVC-based vinyl/HTV materials can be cut WITHOUT lasering them -- laser-cutting vinyl/PVC releases toxic chlorine gas and damages the machine (see safety.md). Never attempt to laser-cut this material even on machines with a laser option; always use Blade Cut mode.

CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE: Blade cut only (power_pct 0 = laser off), per official uk.xtool.com table: 'Self-adhesive Vinyl | 0.3 | Blade cut | M1 10W | 0 / 80 / 1'. Do not laser this material -- self-adhesive vinyl is typically PVC-based and produces toxic chlorine gas plus laser/lens damage if lased. Use the M1's physical blade module exclusively for vinyl materials.

Official uk.xtool.com M1 table row: 'Stainless Steel Necklace | 1.0 | Laser bitmap engraving | M1 5W | 100 / 15 / 1'. Note this is the M1's BLUE DIODE laser (not a fiber module -- M1 has no fiber option), engraving a marking-spray-coated or similarly prepared thin metal surface rather than performing bare-metal fiber marking; diode lasers generally cannot mark bare/uncoated metal as effectively as fiber lasers. Caution: official support.xtool.com/article/1716 warns reflective metal surfaces risk damaging the diode laser module via reflection.

Official P2 FAQ (article 825) states maximum wood cutting thickness is 18mm as a general capability ceiling, not a specific tested recipe -- power/speed/pass values here are a conservative estimate for approaching that maximum thickness, not an official recipe. Always test and expect multiple passes near the machine's rated maximum.

IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE: Official P2 FAQ (article 825) states plywood cutting is capped at 12mm maximum and explicitly warns that cutting plywood THICKER than 12mm 'may cause a fire.' This row represents the maximum officially sanctioned plywood cutting thickness -- do not exceed 12mm plywood thickness on P2 due to xTool's own documented fire risk warning. Power/speed/pass values are an estimate for approaching this threshold safely; always use active fire-watch and never leave the machine unattended near this thickness ceiling (see safety.md).

Official troubleshooting article 2468 ('MDF carbonization/scorching guide') recommends using higher speed with 2 passes rather than low speed/high power/1 pass specifically for MDF to reduce charring and carbonization -- this row reflects that official process guidance. Exact power/speed numeric values are an estimate consistent with that guidance, not a directly published table row.

Official uk.xtool.com table row for Cork Desk Pad, D1 Pro 40W ('Vector engrave | D1 Pro 40W | 15 / 50'), scaled down for D1 Pro 20W module -- treat power percentage as approximate since the module wattage differs from the 40W source row (a lower-wattage module needs somewhat higher power% for an equivalent per-mm2 energy dose, so 20W actual figures may run a few percentage points higher than shown; verify via in-app material test).
Official uk.xtool.com D1 Pro 40W table row: 'Cork Desk Pad | 2mm | Raster engrave | D1 Pro 40W | 8 / 160 (80 Lines per cm)'. 80 lines/cm converted to DPI: 80 x 2.54 = ~203 DPI.
